truth.papers.A51.title: Tailoring Strength and Ductility of an Fe–Mn–Al–C Low-Density Duplex Steel by Controlling the Cooling Path after Hot Rolling
truth.papers.A51.paper_scope: Investigating the effect of cooling path after hot rolling on microstructure and mechanical properties of a low-density duplex Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C steel to achieve a better strength-ductility match via grain refinement and TRIP effect.

prediction.papers.A51.title: Tailoring Strength and Ductility of an Fe–Mn–Al–C Low-Density Duplex Steel by Controlling the Cooling Path after Hot Rolling
prediction.papers.A51.paper_scope: Investigate the effect of post-hot-rolling cooling path (air cooling vs furnace cooling) and subsequent annealing at 800–1000 °C on the microstructure evolution and tensile properties of an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C low-density duplex steel, with emphasis on austenite stability and strength-ductility synergy.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe803mn610al04c.alloys_note: Low-density duplex steel; composition given in wt%.

truth.processes.proc_fc_htr.description: Hot rolled with 7 passes at 900–1100°C to 4 mm thickness after reheating at 1200°C for 2 h, followed by furnace cooling to ambient temperature.

truth.structures.sample_ac_htr.overall_structu: Banded morphology along rolling direction, consisting of δ-ferrite, austenite and martensite.
truth.structures.sample_ac_htr.microstructure_: Banded, with many low-angle grain boundaries due to recovery and recrystallization
Prior austenite bands, partially transformed to martensite
High GND density due to martensitic transformation
Mainly twin martensite, large distortion (dark IQ regions)
truth.structures.sample_fc_htr.overall_structu: Banded δ-ferrite and prior austenite with a small amount of martensite.
truth.structures.sample_fc_htr.microstructure_: Prior austenite bands, small amount of martensite formed
Lower GND density compared to AC
truth.structures.sample_ac_a800.overall_struct: Duplex phase of austenite and ferrite; no martensite. Needle-like α-ferrite embedded in austenite band refines austenite grains.
truth.structures.sample_ac_a800.microstructure: Banded, with many low-angle grain boundaries (length 4351.1 μm)
Needle-like, formed by degradation of prior martensite; refines austenite grains
Film-like between needle-like α-ferrite, refined grains
truth.structures.sample_ac_a900.overall_struct: Duplex austenite and ferrite, similar to AC-A800. Needle-like α-ferrite refines austenite.
truth.structures.sample_ac_a900.microstructure: Banded, low-angle grain boundary length 2685.4 μm
truth.structures.sample_ac_a1000.overall_struc: Duplex austenite-ferrite, with reversed transformation of martensite dominant; small fraction of α-ferrite.
truth.structures.sample_ac_a1000.microstructur: Banded, partially recrystallized, low-angle grain boundary length 1634.3 μm
truth.structures.sample_fc_a800.overall_struct: Banded δ-ferrite, austenite, and small fraction of martensite; some fine equiaxed α-ferrite formed.
truth.structures.sample_fc_a800.microstructure: Banded, with recovery; darker IQ indicating distortion
Fine equiaxed grains within austenite band
Coarse grains, larger than AC annealed samples
Dark regions in austenite band, formed during cooling after annealing
Large distortion as reflected by darker IQ maps
truth.structures.sample_fc_a900.overall_struct: Banded δ-ferrite, austenite, and martensite; some equiaxed α-ferrite.
truth.structures.sample_fc_a1000.overall_struc: Banded δ-ferrite, austenite, and martensite; equiaxed α-ferrite present.

prediction.structures.sample_ac_htr.overall_structu: Banded morphology along rolling direction; alternating δ-ferrite and prior austenite with martensite.
prediction.structures.sample_fc_htr.overall_structu: Banded morphology along rolling direction; alternating δ-ferrite and prior austenite with minor martensite.
prediction.structures.sample_ac_a800.overall_struct: Duplex microstructure of austenite and ferrite with needle-like α-ferrite embedded in austenite bands; δ-ferrite present.
prediction.structures.sample_ac_a900.overall_struct: Duplex microstructure of austenite and ferrite with needle-like α-ferrite; δ-ferrite present.
prediction.structures.sample_ac_a1000.overall_struc: Duplex microstructure of austenite and ferrite; equiaxed recrystallized austenite and recovered/partially recrystallized δ-ferrite.
prediction.structures.sample_fc_a800.overall_struct: Banded morphology composed of δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite formed.
prediction.structures.sample_fc_a900.overall_struct: Banded morphology composed of δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite.
prediction.structures.sample_fc_a1000.overall_struc: Banded morphology composed of δ-ferrite and prior austenite with small amount of martensite; fine equiaxed α-ferrite.

truth.interfaces.sample_fc_a800.defect_interac: Dislocations pile up at the interface, causing strain concentration and cracking; also delamination of banded microstructure.
truth.interfaces.sample_fc_a800.interface_note: Strain concentration at martensite–austenite interface leads to crack initiation.
truth.interfaces.sample_fc_a800.phase_evolutio: During cooling after annealing of furnace-cooled samples, part of austenite transforms to martensite due to lower stability from larger grain size.

prediction.interfaces.sample_fc_a800.defect_interac: Strain concentration at martensite-austenite interface nucleates cracks during deformation.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fc_a800.interface_note: Strain localization at martensite-austenite interface leads to crack initiation and propagation.
prediction.interfaces.sample_fc_a800.phase_evolutio: Martensite forms during final cooling after annealing due to lower austenite stability caused by larger grain size.
prediction.interfaces.sample_ac_a800.interface_note: Needle-like α-ferrite embedded in austenite bands refines austenite grain size and enhances austenite stability.
prediction.interfaces.sample_ac_a800.phase_evolutio: Prior martensite partly degenerates to needle-like α-ferrite during annealing, inheriting the prior martensite morphology.

truth.properties.sample_ac_htr.mechanical.tens: Engineering stress-strain curve in Figure 9a (AC curve); higher strength but lower ductility than FC.
truth.properties.sample_fc_htr.mechanical.tens: Engineering stress-strain curve in Figure 9b (FC curve); lower strength, higher ductility than AC.
truth.properties.sample_ac_a800.mechanical.ten: Figure 9a (curve AC800); strong TRIP effect and work hardening rate recovery at Stage III.
truth.properties.sample_ac_a900.mechanical.ten: Figure 9a (curve AC900); work hardening rate recovery at Stage III.
truth.properties.sample_ac_a1000.mechanical.te: Figure 9a (curve AC1000); work hardening rate recovery at Stage III.
truth.properties.sample_fc_a800.mechanical.ten: Figure 9b (curve FC800); abrupt fracture, no necking.

truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.purpose: Microstructural observation of hot-rolled and annealed samples, and tensile fracture surfaces
truth.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: Revealed banded microstructure, phases (δ-ferrite, austenite, martensite, α-ferrite), needle-like α-ferrite after annealing, dimple fracture in AC-A800 and cleavage-dominated brittle fracture in FC-A800.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.purpose: Phase maps, grain boundary distribution, kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps
truth.characterization_methods.idx_1.key_findi: AC hot-rolled sample: austenite KAM 0.48°, FC: 0.43°; low-angle grain boundary lengths in δ-ferrite decreased with annealing temperature; grain size distributions obtained.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.purpose: Measurement of austenite volume fraction before and after deformation
truth.characterization_methods.idx_2.key_findi: Austenite fractions: AC-A800 58.6%, AC-A900 61.4%, AC-A1000 57.1%; FC-A800 62%, FC-A900 58.6%, FC-A1000 60.6%; after deformation large fraction consumed in AC annealed samples.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.purpose: Evaluation of mechanical properties (strength, ductility, work hardening)
truth.characterization_methods.idx_3.key_findi: Air-cooled annealed samples show higher yield strength (427-513 MPa) and superior elongation (>30%); furnace-cooled annealed samples have lower strength (267-419 MPa) and elongation <20%; work hardening rate recovery observed only in AC annealed samples. Strain rate 10^-3 s^-1, gauge length 50 mm, width 12.5 mm, rolling direction.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_ac_htr.p: Phase mapping, grain boundary distribution and KAM analysis
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_ac_htr.k: Austenite fraction 42 vol%; KAM of austenite 0.48°; high-angle (>15°) and low-angle (>2°) grain boundaries in δ-ferrite.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fc_htr.p: Phase mapping, grain boundary distribution and KAM analysis
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_fc_htr.k: Austenite fraction 51 vol%; KAM of austenite 0.43°; low-angle grain boundaries in banded δ-ferrite.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Estimate Ms temperature considering composition and austenite grain volume to explain presence/absence of martensite after annealing
w_Mn, w_Al, w_C (weight percent); B = 613 μm K^-1; V_γ (austenite grain volume)

Formula: Ms = 692 - 37*w_Mn + 20*w_Al - 502*w_C^(1/2) - B*V_γ^(-1/3). Used to calculate upper limit Ms temperatures for AC annealed samples (24, 1, 34°C respectively) confirming stability of austenite.
Qualitatively explain yield strength enhancement from grain refinement and dislocation density
k (Hall-Petch coefficient), d (grain size), M (Taylor factor), α=0.2, G (shear modulus), b (Burgers vector), ρ (dislocation density)
Δσ_P-H = k*d^(1/2); Δσ_d = M*α*G*b*ρ^(1/2). Grain refinement from 8-10 μm to 3-6 μm and higher GND density contributed to increased yield strength.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Calculated Ms_lim values support experimental observation that almost no martensite forms during final cooling of air-cooled annealed samples.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Martensite start temperature (Ms) equation with grain size term
Estimate upper limit of martensite start temperature to assess austenite stability
B = 613 μm·K^-1; V_γ is austenite grain volume; w_Mn, w_Al, w_C are mass percents
k: Hall-Petch coefficient; d: grain size
M: Taylor factor; α: 0.2 (for fcc); G: shear modulus; b: Burgers vector; ρ: dislocation density
Δσ_d = M α G b ρ^(1/2)

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The studied steel was produced by vacuum induction melting, cast into an ingot, forged at 1050°C and homogenized for 2 h at 1050°C before reheating and hot rolling.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Fracture surface of AC-A800 showed dimples indicating high ductility, while FC-A800 exhibited cracking at martensite–austenite interfaces and cleavage-dominated brittle fracture.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: During annealing of air-cooled samples, carbon and manganese partition into austenite, increasing chemical stability; needle-like α-ferrite further refines austenite, enhancing mechanical stability.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: In furnace-cooled annealed samples, cracks are easily nucleated at the martensite–austenite interface, and delamination of banded microstructure leads to brittle fracture.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: XRD measurements of austenite fraction after deformation: AC-A800 23.7%, AC-A900 44.4%, AC-A1000 46.4%; FC-A800 56.5%, FC-A900 52.4%, FC-A1000 52.1%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: The KAM values of austenite in hot-rolled AC and FC samples are 0.48° and 0.43°, respectively, indicating higher dislocation density in AC due to martensitic transformation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Low-angle grain boundary length in δ-ferrite of AC-A800: 4351.1 μm, AC-A900: 2685.4 μm, AC-A1000: 1634.3 μm, showing progressive recovery with annealing temperature.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: The TRIP effect of austenite played a critical role in the work hardening rate recovery (Stage III) observed only in air-cooled annealed samples, leading to a better strength-ductility match.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Figure 5 (tabular XRD data) reports austenite fractions before and after deformation: AC-A800 58.6%→23.7%, AC-A900 61.4%→44.4%, AC-A1000 57.1%→46.4%, FC-A800 62%→56.5%, FC-A900 58.6%→52.4%, FC-A1000 60.6%→52.1%, indicating pronounced TRIP effect in air-cooled annealed samples.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Figure 9 provides tabular engineering stress–strain data for all hot-rolled and annealed samples; air-cooled annealed samples achieve elongations of 56.9%, 39.5%, and 31.1%, whereas furnace-cooled annealed samples achieve only 19.0%, 15.4%, and 10.6%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Figure 10 provides tabular true stress–strain and work hardening rate data, showing an extra Stage III work hardening rate recovery in air-cooled annealed samples and abrupt fracture without necking in furnace-cooled annealed samples.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: The low-angle grain boundary lengths in ferrite measured by EBSD are 4351.1 μm (AC-A800), 2685.4 μm (AC-A900), and 1634.3 μm (AC-A1000), indicating progressive recovery during annealing.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Fracture surface of AC-A800 exhibits dimples indicative of ductile fracture, while FC-A800 exhibits cleavage-dominated brittle fracture with cracking at martensite–austenite interfaces and delamination of banded microstructure.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: The austenite grain size distributions in Figure 7 show that furnace-cooled annealed samples have much larger austenite grains than air-cooled annealed samples at all annealing temperatures.
